critic_raw:
The SKS multi-task hate speech detection framework claims that joint training with sentiment analysis and category embeddings improves hate speech detection. The multi-task learning assumption carries a specific failure risk.

The core multi-task claim assumes that sentiment analysis is a useful auxiliary task for hate speech detection — specifically that the sentiment signal provides complementary information that improves the hate speech classifier. This is an empirical assumption that can fail if hate speech and sentiment are too highly correlated (making sentiment redundant) or insufficiently correlated (making sentiment noise). The paper does not report the correlation structure between hate speech labels and sentiment labels in the training data. If hate speech is predominantly associated with negative sentiment in the training set, the model learns to detect negative sentiment rather than hate speech specifically, and will fail on positive-sentiment hate speech (e.g., ironic or sarcastic content). Evidence needed: per-sentiment-bucket hate speech detection performance.

The 'category embeddings' from a derogatory word dictionary introduce a lexical matching component into what is otherwise a neural attention model. Derogatory word lists are static and language-community specific: slurs evolve, new terms emerge, and the same words can be reclaimed. The paper should specify the source and date of the dictionary and evaluate performance on examples where hate speech uses no dictionary terms (implicitly hateful language) versus examples where it does — if performance degrades substantially on non-lexical hate speech, the dictionary is doing the work rather than the model.

The model uses a multi-head attention architecture trained with multi-task objectives. Multi-head attention's representation is heavily influenced by the specific tokenization and vocabulary. For hate speech detection, out-of-vocabulary handling (slurs, obfuscated spellings like 'h@te') is critical. If the tokenizer splits obfuscated slurs into subword tokens that are individually innocuous, the attention mechanism cannot detect them. The paper does not characterize performance on obfuscated inputs.

Two benchmark datasets are used. In hate speech detection, dataset-specific performance often reflects the annotation guidelines and demographics of annotators rather than the method's quality — different annotators apply different standards for what constitutes hate speech. Inter-dataset generalization (training on one dataset, testing on another) is the key test of whether the method learns a general hate speech signal versus a dataset-specific annotation signal, and the description does not indicate this cross-dataset evaluation was performed.
all_issues_raised: The correlation structure between hate speech and sentiment labels is unstated; if the training set has predominantly negative-sentiment hate speech, the model may learn to detect negative sentiment rather than hate speech, failing on positive-sentiment or ironic hateful content.

Category embeddings from a static derogatory word dictionary cannot capture evolving slurs or implicitly hateful language without dictionary terms; if performance depends heavily on dictionary matches, the neural component is not doing the work claimed.

Tokenization of obfuscated slurs (e.g., 'h@te') may split them into individually innocuous subword tokens that the attention mechanism cannot recognize as hate speech — this failure mode is not characterized.

Training and evaluation on the same two benchmark datasets tests annotation-specific performance; cross-dataset generalization (train on one, test on another) is necessary to verify that the model learns a general hate speech signal rather than dataset-specific annotator patterns.
all_issues_adjudicated: The correlation structure between hate speech and sentiment labels is unstated; if the training set has predominantly negative-sentiment hate speech, the model may learn to detect negative sentiment rather than hate speech, failing on positive-sentiment or ironic hateful content.

Category embeddings from a static derogatory word dictionary cannot capture evolving slurs or implicitly hateful language without dictionary terms; if performance depends heavily on dictionary matches, the neural component is not doing the work claimed.

Tokenization of obfuscated slurs (e.g., 'h@te') may split them into individually innocuous subword tokens that the attention mechanism cannot recognize as hate speech — this failure mode is not characterized.

Training and evaluation on the same two benchmark datasets tests annotation-specific performance; cross-dataset generalization (train on one, test on another) is necessary to verify that the model learns a general hate speech signal rather than dataset-specific annotator patterns.
